1.synthesis:
Psalm 87 is the Psalter's most concentrated expression of Zion theology expanded to universal scope: the holy mountain where YHWH dwells becomes the birth-register of all nations. The opening declaration — 'On the holy mountain stands the city he founded; the LORD loves the gates of Zion more than all the dwelling places of Jacob' (vv. 1–2) — establishes Zion's unique status through divine love: YHWH loves Zion uniquely among all Israel's dwelling places. The 'glorious things are spoken of you, O city of God' (v. 3) positions the psalm itself as one of those glorious things — the praise of Zion is part of what makes Zion glorious.

The extraordinary universalism of vv. 4–6 — 'Among those who know me I mention Rahab and Babylon; behold, Philistia and Tyre, with Cush — “This one was born there.” And of Zion it shall be said, “This one and that one were born in her”; for the Most High himself will establish her. The LORD records as he registers the peoples, “This one was born there”' — is perhaps the most remarkable universalist text in the OT. YHWH himself records Egypt (Rahab), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush as born in Zion. The traditional enemies of Israel — those who most epitomized the 'nations' in contrast to Israel — are registered as Zion's own citizens. The phrase 'This one was born there' is repeated three times, each time expanding the register.

The concluding vision of v. 7 — 'Singers and dancers alike say, “All my springs are in you”' — images the eschatological worship in Zion: those from all nations who have been registered as born there will join the processional of praise with the declaration that Zion is the source of all their joy. The 'springs' (māʿăyānay) is the image of life-giving water — Zion as the source of living water for the gathered nations.
1.historical_context: The listing of Rahab (Egypt), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush covers the four cardinal directions of the known world from Israel's perspective, suggesting a symbolic comprehensiveness rather than a specific political coalition. That YHWH 'records' these nations as born in Zion uses the language of the civic birth register — a formal administrative act of inclusion. This is citizenship by divine decree, not by geography or ethnicity. The universalism of Psalm 87 stands in continuity with the Abrahamic promise of Gen 12:3 — 'all the families of the earth shall be blessed through you' — and the servant-mission of Isa 49:6.
1.literary_structure: The psalm is compressed — seven verses — but its theological scope is enormous: from the particular (Zion as YHWH's chosen mountain) to the universal (all nations registered as born there) to the eschatological (the gathered nations singing). The triple repetition of 'born there' (vv. 4, 5, 6) is the structural center, accumulating emphasis on the radical claim being made: the nations' birth in Zion is not metaphorical but a divine juridical act registered in YHWH's own record book.
1.theological_application: The radical theology of Psalm 87 — that nations historically hostile to YHWH's people can be registered as born in Zion — is the OT ground for the NT's doctrine of inclusion: 'there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). The citizenship question in Ephesians 2 — 'you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God' (Eph 2:19) — is the NT realization of the Psalm 87 register: those formerly outside are now born in the heavenly Jerusalem.
1.christology: The 'springs' of v. 7 — 'all my springs are in you' — identifies Zion as the source of living water for the gathered nations. This is fulfilled in Christ, who at the Feast of Tabernacles declared: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, “Out of his heart will flow rivers of living water”' (John 7:37–38). Christ himself is the spring of Zion; the gathered nations find their source in him. The registration of the nations as born in Zion is accomplished through regeneration by the Spirit: 'You must be born again' (John 3:7) — reborn into the citizenship of the heavenly Jerusalem (Heb 12:22).
